solution.p: We will factor r^{2}+8r+16 where a=1, b=8, and c=16.

Perfect Square Formula: The formula for perfect square trinomials is (a \pm b)^2=a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2. Note that the polynomial r^{2}+8r+16 matches the formula's right side, and we have: \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+8r+16\amp =\left(r\right)^2+2\left(r\right)\left(4\right)+\left(4\right)^2\newline \amp =\left(r+4\right)\mathopen{}\left(r+4\right)\newline \amp =\left(r+4\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.

For r^{2}+8r+16, since the product of 4 and 4 is 16, and the sum of 4 and 4 is 8, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+8r+16\amp =\left(r+4\right)\mathopen{}\left(r+4\right)\newline \amp =\left(r+4\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (16) = 16.

The number 16 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(16)\amp -1(-16)\amp 2(8)\amp -2(-8)
ewline4(4)\amp -4(-4)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 8, and this sum happens with 4+4=8 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+8r+16\amp =r^{2}+4r+4r+16
ewline \amp =r\mathopen{}\left(r+4ight)+4\mathopen{}\left(r+4ight)
ewline \amp =\left(r+4ight)\mathopen{}\left(r+4ight)
ewline \amp =\left(r+4ight)^{2}\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in r^{2}+8r+16 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 16, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(16)\amp -1(-16)\amp 2(8)\amp -2(-8)\newline4(4)\amp -4(-4)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with r^{2}+8r+16 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:

Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+8r+16\amp =\left(r+4\right)\mathopen{}\left(r+4\right)\newline \amp =\left(r+4\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
